questions.question: Which country is the largest in the world by total land area?
Which river is generally considered the longest in the world?
What is the smallest country in the world by both area and population?
Which mountain is the highest above sea level?
Which country is estimated to have the most islands in the world?
What is the name of the deepest point in the world's oceans?
Which of the Earth's oceans is the largest?
Which continent has the highest number of sovereign countries?
What is the capital city of Australia?
Which is the largest hot desert in the world?
Which of these is the northernmost capital city of a sovereign state?
In which country is Mount Kilimanjaro located?
Which country has the most time zones in the world, including its overseas territories?
Which island is the largest in the world (not including continents)?
The Dead Sea borders Jordan and which other territory?
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean with which other ocean?
What is the capital city of Canada?
In which country is the volcano Mount Vesuvius located?
Which city is currently considered the most populous metropolitan area in the world?
The River Thames flows through which major European city?
What is the capital city of the Netherlands?
Which is the only continent (excluding Antarctica) that has no major deserts?
Monaco is bordered on three sides by which country?
In which country is the Huang He, also known as the Yellow River?
Which is the smallest of the world's five oceans?
Which country has the highest number of active volcanoes?
What is the capital city of Brazil?
By shoreline length, which is considered the largest bay in the world?
Which major river flows through the city of Cairo?
Which is the smallest continent by land area?
What is the capital city of Portugal?
Which sea lies between Great Britain and Norway?
The Gobi Desert is primarily located in China and which other country?
What is the highest mountain on the African continent?
Which European country is famously known as the 'Land of a Thousand Lakes'?
What is the capital city of Vietnam?
Besides Brazil and Ecuador, which other South American country does the equator pass through?
The Strait of Gibraltar separates Europe from which other continent?
Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
What is the capital city of Turkey?
What is the capital city of Kenya?
Which is the largest island in the Mediterranean Sea?
The Galapagos Islands belong to which South American country?
Which country is home to more pyramids than any other country in the world?
What is the capital city of South Korea?
Lake Superior is shared by the United States and which other country?
What is the capital city of New Zealand?
What is the name of the sea that lies between Greece and Turkey?
What is the capital city of Argentina?
Which is the smallest state in the United States by land area?
On which island is the active volcano Mount Etna located?
What is the capital city of Egypt?
Which river flows through the heart of Paris, France?
Which major river flows through the city of Baghdad?
What is the capital city of Thailand?
What is the highest mountain peak in North America?
Which country contains more than half of the world's natural lakes?
What is the capital city of Poland?
Which is the largest island in the Caribbean Sea?
Which sea separates the continents of Africa and Asia?
What is the capital city of Greece?
Which is the largest island in the Indian Ocean?
What is the capital city of Sweden?
The Great Dismal Swamp is located on the border of Virginia and which other US state?
What is the capital city of Iran?
Which river forms a large part of the border between Mexico and the United States?
Which major river flows through the city of Cairo, Egypt?
Which ocean is the smallest and shallowest of the world's five major oceans?
What is the capital of France?
What is the name of the largest ocean on Earth?
What is the currency of the United Kingdom?
Which continent is home to the Amazon Rainforest?
What is the capital of New Zealand?
Which country is the world's largest archipelago state by area and number of islands?
What is the capital city of Austria?
What is the capital city of Ukraine?
What is the capital city of Peru?
Which U.S. state is closest to Africa?
Trick Question: Which U.S. state is the furthest east by longitude?
After Alaska, which U.S. state is closest to Russia?
If you travel directly south from downtown Detroit, Michigan, which country do you enter first?
Which ocean terminal of the Panama Canal sits further west: the Atlantic side or the Pacific side?
What is the closest foreign capital city to the contiguous United States?
Which city is further west: Reno, Nevada or Los Angeles, California?
Which European capital city shares roughly the same latitude as New York City (40 degrees north)?
Which two U.S. states tie for bordering the most other states, with eight borders each?
What is the only U.S. state name that consists of just a single syllable?
What is the only country in South America that has coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea?
What is the largest landlocked country in the world by total land area?
What is the only U.S. state that is 'triple landlocked', requiring you to cross at least three states to reach an ocean?
Which U.S. state is closest to the United Kingdom?
What is the name of the longest river in the world?
With which country does France share its longest continuous land border?
What is the only major river in the world to cross the equator twice?
What is the most populous island in the world, home to over 140 million people?
What is the only U.S. state that shares a land border with exactly one other U.S. state?
What is the largest island located entirely within the Indian Ocean?
Which country spans eleven distinct, consecutive time zones across its mainland?
What is the largest island located in the Mediterranean Sea?
What is the northernmost capital city of a sovereign nation in the world?
What is the large island nation off the eastern coast of mainland Africa?
Russia shares land borders with 14 sovereign nations. Which East Asian country ties this record by also bordering exactly 14 nations?
Which U.S. state contains over 100 active and inactive volcanoes, accounting for roughly 80% of all volcanoes in the country?
Liechtenstein is one of only two 'doubly landlocked' countries in the world (surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries). What is the only other one?
What is the largest island in the world that is not considered its own continent?
What active stratovolcano is the highest mountain peak in Japan?
What is the only Great Lake located entirely within the borders of the United States?
Which U.S. state has the most total miles of coastline, beating the runner-up by over 20,000 miles?
Bordered by Jordan, Israel, and the West Bank, what landlocked salt lake has the lowest elevation on land?
Which major American river carved out the Grand Canyon over millions of years?
What is the most populous country on the continent of Africa?
What narrow body of water separates southern England from northern France and connects the North Sea to the Atlantic Ocean?
What artificial sea-level waterway in Egypt connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea?
Because the Aleutian Islands cross the 180-degree meridian, which U.S. state is technically both the westernmost and easternmost state?
What is the only U.S. state that is 'triple landlocked', meaning a traveler must cross at least three state lines or international borders to reach any ocean?

Constructed as the grand entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair, this towering iron lattice structure on the Champ de Mars has become the definitive architectural symbol of Paris, France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Anthony DELANOIX on Unsplash

questions.question: Perched on a narrow mountain ridge 7,970 feet above sea level, this astonishing 15th-century Incan citadel remained largely hidden from the outside world until its rediscovery by Hiram Bingham in 1911.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eddie Kiszka on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned in 1632 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan to house the tomb of his favorite wife, Mumtaz Mahal, this ivory-white marble mausoleum sits on the southern bank of the Yamuna River in Agra, India.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Annie Spratt on Unsplash

questions.question: Constructed under the Flavian dynasty between 72 AD and 80 AD, this massive freestanding oval amphitheater in the center of Rome once held up to 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial combats.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Köhler on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by the visionary Catalan architect Antoni Gaudí, this breathtaking, organic-styled basilica in Barcelona has been under continuous construction since 1882.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Colin + Meg on Unsplash

questions.question: Famous for its rock-cut architecture and innovative water conduit system, this ancient Nabataean caravan city in Jordan is entered through a narrow sandstone gorge known as the Siq.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Brian Kairuz on Unsplash

questions.question: Originally constructed in the early 12th century as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu before transforming into a Buddhist site, this massive complex in Cambodia is the largest religious monument in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by allPhoto Bangkok on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by Frédéric-Auguste Bartholdi and dedicated in 1886, this colossal copper sculpture on Liberty Island was a gift from the people of France to the United States.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fernando Strabuli on Unsplash

questions.question: Constructed out of reinforced concrete and soapstone between 1922 and 1931, this 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands atop the summit of Corcovado Mountain overlooking Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by gustavo nacht on Unsplash

questions.question: Dominated by the massive step-pyramid known as El Castillo, this large pre-Columbian city built by the Maya civilization sits in the Mexican state of Yucatán.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Christina Abken on Unsplash

questions.question: Engineered by Joseph Strauss and opened in 1937, this iconic suspension bridge painted in a signature 'International Orange' hue spans the one-mile-wide strait connecting San Francisco to Marin County.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Maarten van den Heuvel on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on Salisbury Plain in Wiltshire, England, this prehistoric monument consists of an outer ring of vertical sarsen standing stones topped by horizontal lintels, erected around 2500 BC.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by K. Mitch Hodge on Unsplash

questions.question: Piercing the sky at a staggering 2,717 feet, this neo-Futurist mega-tall skyscraper in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, has reigned as the world's tallest building since its completion in 2010.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Nick Fewings on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon and formally opened in 1973, this Expressionist masterpiece on Bennelong Point features a series of distinctive gleaming white concrete 'shells' resembling sails.
Carved directly out of a single ridge of limestone on the west bank of the Nile, this colossal monolith depicts a mythical creature with the body of a lion and the head of an Egyptian pharaoh.
This ancient rocky citadel rises high above the Greek capital and contains the architectural remains of several historic structures of great universal significance, most notably the Parthenon.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Spencer Davis on Unsplash

questions.question: Sculpted by Gutzon Borglum into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive national memorial features the 60-foot-tall faces of George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Abraham Lincoln.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephen Walker on Unsplash

questions.question: Built in 537 AD as the patriarchal cathedral of the Byzantine Empire's capital, this architectural marvel in Istanbul is world-renowned for its massive, revolutionary pendentive dome.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Nabil Adham on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by Ivan the Terrible in 1555 to commemorate the capture of Kazan, this vivid, geometric cathedral on Moscow's Red Square is famous for its uniquely shaped, brightly painted onion domes.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jaunt and Joy on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by King Frederick William II to represent peace, this 18th-century neoclassical triumphal arch in Berlin stood stranded inside the exclusion zone of the Berlin Wall during the Cold War.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Florian Wehde on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in southwestern Colorado, this rugged National Park protects over 600 ancient Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings, including the massive 150-room Cliff Palace built under overhanging rock ledges.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Wallace Bentt on Unsplash

questions.question: Serving as the imperial palace for twenty-four emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties, this massive, walled palace complex in the center of Beijing contains 980 surviving buildings across 180 acres.
Designed principally by Donato Bramante, Michelangelo, Carlo Maderno, and Gian Lorenzo Bernini, this Renaissance masterpiece in Vatican City stands as the largest church building in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fabio Fistarol on Unsplash

questions.question: Construction began in 1173 on this freestanding bell tower in Tuscany, Italy, which famously began to sink into its soft alluvial subsoil before the third of its eight stories was even completed.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Kaden on Unsplash

questions.question: Originally built as a humble hunting lodge, this sprawling, opulent chateau was transformed by King Louis XIV into the seat of absolute political power and courtly life in France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mathias Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Piercing the skyline at 1,815 feet, this concrete communications and observation tower in downtown Toronto, Canada, reigned as the world's tallest freestanding structure from 1975 until 2007.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by adriana macias on Unsplash

questions.question: Home to the historic Djinguereber Mosque built from mud bricks and wood in 1327, this ancient trading hub in Mali flourished as a legendary center of Islamic scholarship under the Mali Empire.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Laurent Gence on Unsplash

questions.question: Opened in 1914 after a grueling, disease-plagued construction effort by France and the United States, this artificial 51-mile waterway utilizes a system of stepped locks to bisect the Isthmus of Panama.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alex Pagliuca on Unsplash

questions.question: Housed inside a massive 12th-century fortress turned royal palace along the Seine River, this institution features a controversial 1989 glass pyramid entrance designed by I.M. Pei.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michael Fousert on Unsplash

questions.question: Rising to a symmetrical, snow-capped cone of 12,389 feet, this active stratovolcano southwest of Tokyo stands as an immortal cultural and spiritual symbol of Japan.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: This stunning tidal island and medieval Benedictine abbey off the coast of Normandy, France, is completely cut off from the mainland at high tide, reachable only via a winding causeway.
Completed in a blistering 13 months during the Great Depression, this 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan held the title of the world's tallest building for nearly 40 years.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kit Suman on Unsplash

questions.question: Discovered accidentally by local farmers digging a well in 1974, this massive collection of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers was buried in Xi'an to protect China's first emperor in the afterlife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Aaron Greenwood on Unsplash

questions.question: Officially renamed Elizabeth Tower in 2012, this neo-Gothic clock tower at the north end of the Houses of Parliament in London is universally known by the nickname of its massive, 13.7-ton main bell.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Marcin Nowak on Unsplash

questions.question: Also known as the Wailing Wall, this ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem was originally constructed by Herod the Great to support the outer courtyard of the Second Temple.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by JR Ross on Unsplash

questions.question: Constructed between 1609 and 1616 during the rule of Ahmed I, this historic Istanbul mosque is famous for its cascading domes, six minarets, and interior walls hand-painted with over 20,000 blue Iznik tiles.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Rodrigo on Unsplash

questions.question: Running for roughly 9 miles along the Atlantic coast of County Clare, these breathtaking, vertical shale and sandstone sea cliffs plunge up to 702 feet into the ocean as Ireland's most visited natural landmark.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jarek Skowron on Unsplash

questions.question: Built as the futuristic centerpiece of the 1962 Century 21 Exposition World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a flying-saucer-shaped observation deck dominating the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Andrea Leopardi on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by Napoleon in 1806 following his victory at Austerlitz, this monumental neoclassical arch stands proudly at the center of the Place Charles de Gaulle at the western end of the Champs-Élysées.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by ian kelsall on Unsplash

questions.question: Comprising a vast mix of decayed temples, chapels, and pylons, this massive open-air complex near Luxor, Egypt, is dominated by the awe-inspiring Great Hypostyle Hall, which contains 134 colossal sandstone columns.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mo Gabrail on Unsplash

questions.question: Spelling out its name in 45-foot-tall white capital letters across Mount Lee in the Santa Monica Mountains, this landmark was originally erected in 1923 as a temporary advertisement for a local real estate development.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by De'Andre Bush on Unsplash

questions.question: Located at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue in Washington, D.C., this iconic building serves as the official residence and workplace of the President of the United States.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Everett Strickler on Unsplash

questions.question: Visible from many aerial and satellite views, this ancient stone-and-brick fortification stretches thousands of miles across the northern borders of China to protect against historic invasions.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Craig Nagy (Frayed), Vancouver, Canada via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Carved out over millions of years by the Colorado River in Arizona, this immense, colorful, and deep natural gorge stands as one of the world's most famous natural wonders.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Luca Galuzzi (Lucag) via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Straddling the border between New York state and Ontario, Canada, this massive and thunderous group of three waterfalls is a world-famous destination for sightseers and boat tours.

Nicknamed 'The Crossroads of the World', this brightly illuminated commercial intersection in Midtown Manhattan, New York City, is famous for its massive digital billboards and annual New Year's Eve ball drop.
Built as massive stone tombs for ancient Egyptian pharaohs like Khufu, these three towering structures have stood on a desert plateau outside Cairo for over 4,500 years.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Ricardo Liberato via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Spanning 843 acres in the middle of Manhattan, this massive, world-famous urban park features sprawling lawns, artificial lakes, walking paths, and a famous zoo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Jet Lowe via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and China, this towering, snow-capped peak holds the title of the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Photo by Pavel Novak via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Featuring 775 rooms and the famous 'Changing of the Guard' ceremony, this massive London estate serves as the official administrative headquarters of the British monarch.
Standing on the South Bank of the River Thames, this massive, 443-foot-tall giant observation wheel offers tourists sweeping, panoramic views of London's skyline.
This famous four-mile stretch of South Las Vegas Boulevard in Nevada is world-renowned for its concentration of massive resort hotels, bright neon lights, and sprawling casinos.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Matthew Field, http://www.photography.mattfield.com via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: This active stratovolcano with a perfectly symmetrical, snow-capped cone stands as the tallest mountain in Japan and a legendary symbol of the country.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: Mainly located in Wyoming, this legendary wilderness area became the world's very first National Park in 1872 and is famous for its wildlife and the 'Old Faithful' geyser.
This famous freestanding bell tower in Italy is known worldwide because its weak foundations caused it to noticeably tilt to one side shortly after construction began.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Kaden on Unsplash

questions.question: This colossal, 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands with his arms wide open atop Corcovado Mountain, looking down over the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by gustavo nacht on Unsplash

questions.question: Painted in a bright 'International Orange' color to stand out in the fog, this massive suspension bridge spans the opening of San Francisco Bay in California.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Maarten van den Heuvel on Unsplash

questions.question: This giant stone oval amphitheater in the center of Rome, Italy, was built by the ancient Roman Empire to hold massive gladiatorial games and public spectacles.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Köhler on Unsplash

questions.question: This iconic, white marble palace-like monument in Agra, India, was built by a grieving Mughal emperor in the 1600s to serve as a stunning tomb for his favorite wife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Annie Spratt on Unsplash

questions.question: Though the name technically refers only to the massive 13-ton bell inside, this iconic, historic clock tower at the Houses of Parliament is a universal symbol of London.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Marcin Nowak on Unsplash

questions.question: Spelled out in massive 45-foot-tall white letters across Mount Lee in California, this landmark is the ultimate visual symbol of the American film industry.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by De'Andre Bush on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on the harbor in Australia's largest city, this iconic performing arts building is famous for its unique roof design, which looks like giant white sails or shells.
This towering, 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its open-air observation deck and for being climbed by King Kong in classic cinema.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kit Suman on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in a grassy field in Wiltshire, England, this famous prehistoric mystery consists of a circular ring of massive standing stones erected thousands of years ago.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by K. Mitch Hodge on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous, narrow waterway connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea, separating Spain from Morocco.
Located on an island in the Seine River, this iconic, medieval Catholic cathedral in Paris is famous for its Gothic architecture, gargoyles, and flying buttresses.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Hannah Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Situated on a rocky island in San Francisco Bay, this notorious former federal prison once held famous criminals like Al Capone.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Photographed by and copyright of (c) David Corby (User:Miskatonic, uploader) 2006. Cropped by Dr. Blofeld via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: This famous artificial waterway cuts through Central America, allowing ships to pass directly between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Angel Silva on Unsplash

questions.question: As the world's largest art museum, this former Parisian palace features a famous glass pyramid entrance and houses the Mona Lisa.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michael Fousert on Unsplash

questions.question: Bordered by Jordan and Israel, this famous saltwater lake sits at the lowest elevation on land and is so salty that swimmers float effortlessly.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: David Shankbone via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Sculpted into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive monument features the 60-foot heads of four American presidents.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephen Walker on Unsplash

questions.question: Often confused with London Bridge, this iconic combined bascule and suspension bridge crosses the River Thames next to the Tower of London.
Hidden high in the Andes Mountains of Peru, this world-famous 15th-century Incan citadel is often called the 'Lost City of the Incas'.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eddie Kiszka on Unsplash

questions.question: Though mostly demolished in 1989, preserved concrete stretches of this historical barrier still stand as a major landmark in Germany's capital city.
Located on Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula, this ancient Mayan city is dominated by a massive, world-famous step-pyramid known as El Castillo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Christina Abken on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, incredibly opulent palace complex outside of Paris was built by the 'Sun King' Louis XIV and features the famous Hall of Mirrors.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mathias Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Standing tall near Tokyo, this active stratovolcano is Japan's highest peak and is instantly recognizable by its symmetrical, snow-capped cone.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: Surrounded entirely by the city of Rome, this tiny walled independent city-state serves as the spiritual headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church.
Running through the heart of Manhattan's Theater District, this famous New York street is globally synonymous with commercial theater and massive musical productions.
Located in the Scottish Highlands, this deep, murky freshwater lake is famous worldwide due to alleged sightings of a mythical aquatic monster.
Built for the 1962 World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a saucer-shaped observation deck that dominates the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Andrea Leopardi on Unsplash

questions.question: Visible from space off the northeastern coast of Australia, this massive natural wonder is the largest coral reef system on Earth.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: NASA, by MISR via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located inside Vatican City, this massive Renaissance-era church is one of the largest in the world and features a giant dome designed by Michelangelo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fabio Fistarol on Unsplash

questions.question: Located near Naples, Italy, this active volcano is famous for its catastrophic eruption in 79 AD that buried the ancient Roman city of Pompeii.
Located in Lower Manhattan, this short, historic street is globally recognized as the definitive geometric and cultural hub of American financial markets.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Daniel Lloyd Blunk-Fernández on Unsplash

questions.question: Connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn over the East River, this beautiful historic suspension bridge features iconic stone arches and a popular pedestrian walkway.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alexander Rotker on Unsplash

questions.question: Stretching across the entire northern third of the African continent, this massive, arid landscape holds the title of the largest hot desert in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Wolfgang Hasselmann on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, rectangular urban park in San Francisco is even larger than New York's Central Park and stretches all the way to the Pacific Ocean.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jeffrey Eisen on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in Tanzania, this massive, snow-capped dormant volcano stands as the highest mountain in Africa and the tallest free-standing mountain in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephan Bechert on Unsplash

questions.question: This world-famous upscale shopping street in Beverly Hills, California, is legendary for its ultra-luxury designer boutiques and celebrity sightings.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tjerk van der Heijden on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous neoclassical triumphal arch stands proudly at the center of a massive traffic roundabout at the western end of the Champs-Élysées in Paris.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by ian kelsall on Unsplash

questions.question: This world-famous, winding Italian waterway serves as the main aquatic 'street' for gondolas and water buses traveling through the city of Venice.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tomasz Zielonka on Unsplash

questions.question: This historic Spanish mission and fortress complex in San Antonio, Texas, was the site of a legendary 1836 battle during the Texas Revolution.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Gabriel Tovar on Unsplash

questions.question: Dedicated to the goddess Athena, the majestic ruins of this ancient white marble temple dominate the rocky cliffside of the Acropolis in Athens, Greece.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Spencer Davis on Unsplash

questions.question: Painted in striking white and international orange zones to comply with aviation laws, this landmark Eiffel-inspired lattice tower stands tall in Japan's capital.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Louie Martinez on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, rectangular central plaza in the heart of Madrid, Spain, is completely surrounded by historic three-story residential buildings with arches.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eduardo Rodriguez on Unsplash

questions.question: Serving as the massive headquarters for the U.S. Department of Defense in Virginia, what building is famous for its unique five-sided geometric design.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: David B. Gleason from Chicago, IL via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Constructed along the Atlantic coast of Florida, this sprawling historical launch site serves as the ultimate primary hub for American human spaceflight.
This historic, open central square in Moscow sits next to the Kremlin and features the brightly colored onion domes of St. Basil's Cathedral. What is it called?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alexey Fedotov on Unsplash

questions.question: This iconic, active volcano on the island of Sicily stands as the tallest active volcano in Europe and is in a near-constant state of activity.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Asa Rodger on Unsplash

questions.question: This ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem is the holiest site where Jews are permitted to freely gather and pray.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by JR Ross on Unsplash

questions.question: Stretching across fifteen blocks of Hollywood Boulevard in California, this landmark sidewalk features thousands of five-pointed terrazzo and brass stars.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Meg von Haartman on Unsplash

questions.question: This spectacular, giant marble fountain in Rome is world-famous for the tourist tradition of throwing a coin over your shoulder to ensure a return trip.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michele Bitetto on Unsplash

questions.question: Located inside the Apostolic Palace in Vatican City, this historic chapel is world-famous for its magnificent ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Calvin Craig on Unsplash

questions.question: Spanning the southern tip of Florida, this massive, subtropical marshland wilderness is famous for its unique ecosystem and wild alligators.
This historic complex of commercial buildings in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its massive sunken plaza, ice rink, and annual giant Christmas tree.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kaydn Ito on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous landmark is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jovyn Chamb on Unsplash

questions.question: Name the ancient structure pictured here.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Davi Pimentel on Unsplash

questions.question: What ancient citadel is pictured here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Willian Justen de Vasconcellos on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous structure is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Max van den Oetelaar on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous clock tower is pictured here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jurica Koletić on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous performing arts venue is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Dan Freeman on Unsplash

questions.question: What ancient city carved into rose-red cliffs is shown here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Torsten Dederichs on Unsplash

questions.question: Straddling the border of Zambia and Zimbabwe, this waterfall on the Zambezi River is the largest in the world by total area of falling water.
At 9,573 feet, this is the highest mountain in Greece and, in ancient Greek religion, the home of the twelve Olympian gods.
This ancient Roman city in southern Italy was catastrophically buried under volcanic ash and pumice following the eruption of a nearby volcano in 79 AD, preserving it nearly intact.
Straddling the border of France and Italy at 15,774 feet, this is the highest peak in the Alps and in all of Western Europe.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Tinelot Wittermans via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Opened in 1869 and connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea, this 120-mile Egyptian waterway dramatically shortened shipping routes between Europe and Asia.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by samuel hanna on Unsplash

questions.question: This volcanic archipelago in the Pacific Ocean, belonging to Ecuador, was visited by Charles Darwin in 1835 and inspired his theory of natural selection through its unique wildlife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Bruce on Unsplash

questions.question: Though slightly shorter than the Nile, this South American river carries more water than any other river on Earth and flows through the world's largest rainforest.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Ivars Utināns on Unsplash

questions.question: Straddling the border of Peru and Bolivia at an elevation of 12,507 feet, this is the world's highest navigable lake and the largest lake in South America by surface area.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by WILLIAN REIS on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous geyser in Yellowstone National Park earned its name from its consistent eruption schedule, shooting boiling water up to 184 feet in the air roughly every 60 to 110 minutes.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Emily Campbell on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in southern Utah, this national park is known for its hoodoos — tall, thin spires of rock formed by erosion — and for having the world's largest concentration of these unique geological formations.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tim Golder on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in eastern Utah, this national park contains over 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the famous Delicate Arch which appears on Utah's license plate.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Ken Cheung on Unsplash

questions.question: This national park in southwestern Utah is known for its steep red cliffs, The Narrows slot canyon, and Angels Landing — a trail that requires navigating chains bolted into sheer rock.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jamie Hagan on Unsplash

questions.question: Formed by the collapse of a volcano around 5,700 years ago, this lake in Oregon is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,943 feet and is renowned for its startling deep blue color.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jeff Hopper on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on the northeastern coast of Northern Ireland, this area of about 40,000 interlocking basalt columns — most of them hexagonal — was formed by an ancient volcanic eruption.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Sean Kuriyan on Unsplash

questions.question: This stretch of coastline along the southern edge of Italy's Sorrentine Peninsula is famous for its dramatic cliffs, pastel-colored towns perched above the sea, and UNESCO World Heritage status.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tom Podmore on Unsplash

questions.question: These iconic chalk cliffs on the English coast near the Strait of Dover reach heights of up to 350 feet and have long served as a symbol of Britain due to their visibility from France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heather Wang on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in Siberia, Russia, this is the world's deepest lake at 5,387 feet and holds approximately 20% of the world's unfrozen surface fresh water.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Daria Gordova on Unsplash

questions.question: What is the only letter in the English alphabet that is not in the name of any U.S. state?
Which continent is the largest by land area?
Which desert is the largest hot desert in the world?
What is the capital city of Japan?
Which city is famous for its leaning tower?
Which continent is home to the Sahara Desert?
What is the primary language spoken in Brazil?
What is the currency of Japan?
What is the capital city of France?
Which US state contains the overwhelming majority of the Grand Canyon National Park, including its famous South Rim?
Which US state contains the massive subtropical wilderness of the Everglades National Park within its borders?
Which US state contains Denali, the highest mountain peak in North America?
Which US state contains the dormant volcano Mauna Kea, home to many of the world's premier astronomical observatories?
Which US state contains the historic Alamo mission building within the city of San Antonio?
Which US state contains Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the United States?
Which US state contains the striking eroded geological formations of Badlands National Park?
Which US state contains Mammoth Cave National Park, which preserves the world's longest known cave system?
The majority of the historic Okefenokee Swamp is located within the borders of which US state?
Which US state contains Acadia National Park, primarily located on Mount Desert Island?
While the Colorado River runs through it, the physical structure of the Hoover Dam itself anchors directly into Arizona and which other US state?
Which US state contains the iconic Space Needle observation tower, built for the 1962 World's Fair?
Which US state contains the world headquarters of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in Salt Lake City?
Which US state contains the 630-foot-tall stainless steel Gateway Arch monument in the city of St. Louis?
Which US state contains the historic, jazz-filled French Quarter neighborhood within its largest city?
Which US state contains the wilderness of Isle Royale National Park, located entirely within Lake Superior?
Which US state contains Independence Hall, the historic building where the US Declaration of Independence was debated and adopted?
Which US state contains the massive stone carvings of four US Presidents at Mount Rushmore?
Which US state contains the affluent summer colony island of Martha's Vineyard?
Which US state contains the 200-mile string of barrier islands known as the Outer Banks?
Which US state contains the towering granite cliffs of El Capitan and Half Dome within Yosemite National Park?
Which US state contains the global corporate headquarters of Walmart Inc. in the city of Bentonville?
While it spills slightly into Idaho and Montana, roughly 96 percent of Yellowstone National Park's land area sits inside which US state?
Which US state hosts the famous Indianapolis 500 automobile race at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway every May?
Which US state contains the entirety of the Green Mountains range, which heavily inspires its official state nickname?
While Yellowstone is famous for its geysers, which US state contains the stark, terraced travertine structures of Mammoth Hot Springs?
Which US state contains Lake Itasca, the small glacial body of water officially recognized as the primary headwaters of the Mississippi River?
Which US state contains the 150-mile-long Garlock Fault, a major left-lateral strike-slip geological fault that intersects the San Andreas Fault?
Which US state contains Fort Sumter, the sea fort where the first shots of the American Civil War were fired in 1861?
Which US state contains the city of Tupelo, famous for preserving the tiny two-room birthplace home of Elvis Presley?
Which US state contains Isle au Haut, a rugged island that holds a distinct, detached section of Acadia National Park?
Which US state contains Lake Pontchartrain, which is spanned by the world's longest continuous bridge over water?
Which US state contains the Great Serpent Mound, a 1,348-foot-long prehistoric effigy mound depicting a snake swallowing an egg?
Which US state contains Copper Harbor, a northern settlement that serves as the official terminus of US Route 41?
Which US state contains the majority of the Suwannee River's headwaters within the protected Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge?
Which US state contains the historic Dripping Springs Natural Area, tucked into the rugged Organ Mountains?
Which US state contains the Bacon Ridge Natural Area, a protected wildlife corridor located within Anne Arundel County?
Which US state contains the Tallgrass Prairie National Preserve, which protects the last vast expanse of the original tallgrass ecosystem?
Which US state contains Mount Mitchell, the highest peak in the Appalachian Mountains and the highest peak in the eastern United States?
Which US state contains Door County, a major tourist peninsula nestled between Green Bay and Lake Michigan?
Which US state contains the Barr Trail, a challenging 13-mile mountain path that ascends directly to the summit of Pikes Peak?
Which US state contains Block Island, an offshore landmass bounded by the Atlantic Ocean and a sound named for the state itself?
Which US state contains the city of Coeur d'Alene, situated on a stunning glacial lake of the same name?
While the lake touches New York and Canada, which US state borders the eastern shoreline of Lake Champlain?
While they extend into Oklahoma, the towering Hot Springs National Park sits within the portion of the Ouachita Mountains located in which US state?
Which US state shares the Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area along its eastern river border with New Jersey?
Which US state contains Chimney Rock, a towering prominent rock formation that served as a critical landmark for pioneers on the Oregon Trail?
Which US state contains Theodore Roosevelt National Park, where the Little Missouri River flows through the colorful badlands?
Which US state was the site of the Battle of Antietam in 1862, which remains the bloodiest single-day battle in American military history?
Which US state hosted the 1787 Constitutional Convention, where the United States Constitution was drafted and signed in Philadelphia?
The discovery of the Comstock Lode, the first major silver ore deposit in the United States, accelerated the statehood process for which US state?
Which US state was the center of a brutal series of violent civil confrontations in the 1850s over the legality of slavery, earning it a famous 'bloody' moniker?
The Great Swamp Fight, a pivotal and devastating engagement of King Philip's War in 1675, took place in which modern-day US state?
Which US state was the location of the 1886 Haymarket Affair, a historic labor protest bombing?
Which US state was the primary setting for Shays' Rebellion, an armed uprising of farmers from 1786 to 1787 that highlighted the weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation?
The historic federal armory at Harpers Ferry, targeted by abolitionist John Brown for an armed slave revolt in 1859, is located in which modern-day US state?
The mysterious 'Lost Colony' of Roanoke Island, established by Sir Walter Raleigh in 1587, was located off the coast of which modern-day US state?
Which US state constructed the 363-mile Erie Canal, completing it in 1825 to connect the Hudson River directly to Lake Erie?
Which US state hosted the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848, universally recognized as the first women's rights convention in the country?
The 1832 Battle of Bad Axe, the tragic final clash of the Black Hawk War, took place along the banks of the Mississippi River in which US state?
Which US state was the epicenter of the Whiskey Rebellion in the 1790s, forcing President George Washington to personally lead a militia to quell the tax revolt?
The historic 1894 Pullman Strike, a nationwide railroad boycott that completely paralyzed rail traffic, originated in a company town located in which US state?
Which US state was the location of Bacon's Rebellion in 1676, an armed uprising of settlers that resulted in the burning of Jamestown?
The 1892 Homestead Strike, a violent confrontation between striking steelworkers and Pinkerton detectives, took place at an Andrew Carnegie steel mill in which US state?
Which US state was the site of the 1957 integration crisis involving the 'Little Rock Nine' at Central High School?
The late-19th-century Ghost Dance religious movement, founded by the Northern Paiute prophet Wovoka, originated in which US state?
The catastrophic Johnstown Flood of 1889, which claimed over 2,200 lives after the failure of the South Fork Dam, occurred in which US state?
The 1853 Gadsden Purchase secured thousands of square miles of southern land along the Mexican border to finalize the territory of New Mexico and which other US state?
The 1811 Battle of Tippecanoe, a conflict between American forces and Tecumseh's Native American confederacy, was fought in which modern-day US state?
Which US state was the political home of Colonial Williamsburg, which served as the capital of the colony from 1699 to 1780?
Which US state is home to the primary Ben & Jerry's ice cream factory in Waterbury, famous for its 'Flavor Graveyard' tracking retired flavors?
Which US state contains a museum in Blackfoot shaped like a giant baked potato, celebrating the agricultural crop that anchors the region's economy?
Which US state contains the official SPAM Museum, celebrating the famous canned meat product manufactured by Hormel Foods in Austin?
Which US state contains Avery Island, the salt dome landmass where every single bottle of Tabasco sauce is produced?
Which US state contains the Just Born candy factory, which manufactures nearly all of the country's marshmallow Peeps?
Which US state contains a massive, tourist-heavy creamery facility along its rugged Pacific Northwest coastline famous for its medium cheddar and coastal berry ice creams?
Which US state contains the global corporate headquarters of John Deere?
Which US state contains the massive, sprawling global corporate campus of Nike?
Which US state contains the Everett Factory, a massive aerospace production building that holds the record as the largest building in the world by volume?
Which US state contains the Bowling Green Assembly Plant, the exclusive global manufacturing home of the Chevrolet Corvette sports car?
Which US state contains the historic corporate headquarters of Kellogg's in Battle Creek, earning the settlement the nickname 'Cereal City'?
Which US state contains the Mall of America, the largest shopping mall and indoor theme park complex in the United States?
Which US state looks to the future with its concise, single-word official motto, 'Forward'?
Which US state boasts the only Greek-word official motto in the country, 'Eureka' (I have found it), referencing its famous mid-19th-century gold rush?

Though its official Latin motto translates to 'The welfare of the people shall be the supreme law,' which midwestern state is universally known by its unofficial, skeptical motto, the 'Show-Me State'?
Adopted in 1945 from a Revolutionary War toast written by General John Stark, 'Live Free or Die' is the assertively independent motto of which New England state?
Derived from a Caddo Native American word meaning 'friends' or 'allies,' which large southern state uses the simple, single-word official motto 'Friendship'?
Which US state passed a unique statute explicitly establishing the 'Aloha Spirit' as a legal obligation and philosophical motto for its officials and citizens?
Meaning 'Ever upward' or 'Still higher,' the Latin motto 'Excelsior' is prominently featured on the flag and coat of arms of which eastern state?
Which US state features the classic philosophical Latin motto 'Esse quam videri,' which translates into English as 'To be, rather than to seem'?
Chosen during a 1967 centennial celebration to represent a land of geographic opportunity, 'North to the Future' is the official motto of which US state?
Which state honors its pioneering trailblazers with the lyrical, independent motto 'Alis volat propriis,' translated as 'She flies with her own wings'?
As the first territory and state to grant women the right to vote, hold office, and serve on juries, which western state appropriately claims the official motto 'Equal Rights'?
Admitted to the Union during the American Civil War, which western state references its wartime origin on its state seal with the patriotic motto 'All for Our Country'?
Representing the hard work and cooperation of its early settlers, which western state uses the single-word motto 'Industry' alongside the visual symbol of a beehive?
Which US state officially adopted the transit-themed motto 'The Crossroads of America' due to the massive volume of interstate highways and railroads intersecting within its borders?
Reflecting its deep ties to mining and the mineral wealth of its mountains, which state uses the bilingual Spanish motto 'Oro y Plata' (Gold and Silver)?
Which southern state pairs its official motto, 'United we stand, divided we fall,' with an image of two men—traditionally a statesman and a frontiersman—embracing on its state seal?

Taking inspiration from its unique, water-locked geography, which Great Lakes state uses the Latin motto 'Si quaeris peninsulam amoenam, circumspice' (If you seek a pleasant peninsula, look about you)?
Which western state uses the calm, optimistic, and slightly colloquial timeline motto 'By and By'?
Which southern state perfectly summarizes its economic foundation on its state seal with the dual-concept motto 'Agriculture and Commerce'?
As the first state to ratify the United States Constitution, which Mid-Atlantic state boasts the foundational motto 'Liberty and Independence'?
Which midwestern agricultural state boldly declares, 'Our liberties we prize and our rights we will maintain,' across a streaming ribbon held by an eagle on its state flag?

Reflecting its rugged topography and its secession from a neighboring territory during the Civil War, which Appalachian state uses the Latin motto 'Montani semper liberi' (Mountaineers are always free)?
Which southern state features two distinct Latin mottos on its seal: 'Animis opibusque parati' (Prepared in mind and resources) and the famous 'Dum spiro spero' (While I breathe, I hope)?
Looking back at its steady territorial expansion and progress, which southwestern state uses the dynamic Latin motto 'Crescit eundo,' translating into English as 'It grows as it goes'?

Following a high-profile legal challenge in the 1990s regarding the separation of church and state, federal courts upheld the official biblical motto, 'With God, all things are possible,' for which midwestern state?
In which northern US state is it a long-standing (though rarely enforced) misdemeanor to push a live caribou out of an airplane?

Which midwestern state hosted a quirky historical event in 1993 where lawmakers legally designated milk as the official state beverage, only for the governor to sign the bill into law while drinking a glass of chocolate milk?
Which southern state has a lottery system dedicated exclusively to awarding a limited number of hunting tags for alligators each year due to the sheer population density of the reptiles?
In a bizarre structural acquisition, a real estate developer purchased the actual, historic London Bridge from England in 1968 and rebuilt it brick-by-brick in which desert US state?
While famous for its massive open plains and fields, which midwestern state oddly contains a point where you can stand on an island, in a lake, on an island, in a lake?
Which state has an official state soil named 'Tama silt loam' that is so highly valued for agriculture it was celebrated with its own dedicated continuous exhibition at the state capitol?
Which US state boasts an unusual geological footprint by containing a mountain range that is completely circular, known as the Sutter Buttes, often called the world's smallest mountain range?
Which western state features an official annual legislative recess specifically so lawmakers can attend a historic, county-fair frog jumping jubilee inspired by a Mark Twain short story?

Which midwestern state is uniquely split between the Eastern and Central time zones, resulting in a confusing quirk where driving across certain county lines shifts your clock by an hour without leaving the state region?
Which eastern state contains the borough of Centralia, where a subterranean coal mine fire has been burning completely out of control beneath the streets since 1962?
Which US state features a historic municipality where it was once technically illegal to let your pet flamingo wander into a local barber shop?

Which northeast state contains a state park where thousands of authentic prehistoric footprints are preserved, discovered entirely by accident when a bulldozer operator was clearing land for a state building?
Which coastal New England state features a historic law on the books stating that any whale found dead on the shore belongs technically to the governor, specifically for its blubber?
Which midwestern plains state contains 'Carhenge,' a quirky, full-scale replica of England's Stonehenge constructed entirely out of vintage American automobiles painted gray?
Which northeast state has a famous blue law stating that a pickle cannot legally be considered a pickle unless it can bounce when dropped from a height of one foot?
Which eastern state contained the fictional 'paper town' of Agloe, which was invented by mapmakers to catch copyright violators but actually became a real place when a general store was built there?
In 1893, the Supreme Court ruled in a case originating from a port in which state that a tomato is legally a vegetable rather than a fruit for tax and tariff purposes?
Which southwestern desert state hosts a massive annual festival dedicated entirely to ostrich racing, where riders attempt to navigate chariots pulled by the giant birds?
While New York drops a crystal ball on New Year's Eve, which western state celebrates the midnight countdown by dropping a massive, glowing, manufactured replica of a potato from a crane?
